metadata.seed: Yeah. Okay. I guess you guessed. Yeah. Um. Well, I guess I'll tell you, but I, I'm kind of embarrassed to tell you, but, so, like, I'm in college, and all my friends are dating, and I'm just really, like, nervous to just tell you, but, like, I really haven't dated before, and I'm really nervous and scared to date, and I just I just don't know how to do it, and I don't know. I'm just nervous.

patient.query: Yeah. Okay. I guess you guessed. Yeah. Um. Well, I guess I'll tell you, but I, I'm kind of embarrassed to tell you, but, so, like, I'm in college, and all my friends are dating, and I'm just really, like, nervous to just tell you, but, like, I really haven't dated before, and I'm really nervous and scared to date, and I just I just don't know how to do it, and I don't know. I'm just nervous.

automatic_thoughts: I haven't dated before
I don't know how to date
I'm embarrassed to tell you

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a mix of embarrassment and nervousness about stepping into dating, especially since everyone else seems to be moving forward. It’s understandable to feel uncertain when you haven’t had that experience before. You’re not alone in feeling this way, and it’s okay to take your time figuring it out.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: fear of saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: entailment

patient.query: Yeah, it’s just... I can’t help but feel like I’m falling behind, you know? Like everyone’s got their life together and I’m just here, still trying to figure out basic things. It’s really embarrassing when I think about it. What if I completely mess up?

automatic_thoughts: I’m falling behind
Everyone’s got their life together
I’m still trying to figure out basic things
It’s really embarrassing
What if I completely mess up?

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and worried that others are ahead while you’re still figuring things out. That can feel really embarrassing and scary. Many people experience that sense of falling behind at some point.

patient.query: Yeah, it really is scary. I just keep thinking, "Why can’t I just be like everyone else?" Sometimes I feel like I’m the only one struggling, and then I start to doubt if I’m even capable of doing anything right. It’s exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: Why can’t I just be like everyone else?
I’m the only one struggling
I doubt if I’m even capable of doing anything right

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling alone in this struggle and doubting your abilities. What evidence do you have that others aren’t also facing similar challenges? How might you view this situation if you considered that many people experience moments of doubt? What could be a more balanced way to think about your strengths and the difficulties you’re encountering?

patient.query: I guess... I know deep down that everyone has their own issues, but when I’m in my head, it feels so personal. It’s like I’m on this stage where everyone can see my mistakes, and I’m just waiting to mess up again. I want to believe I have strengths, but I can’t help but focus on what I’m not doing right. It’s just hard to see the good when I feel so overwhelmed.

automatic_thoughts: everyone has their own issues
it feels so personal
I’m on this stage where everyone can see my mistakes
I’m just waiting to mess up again
I can’t help but focus on what I’m not doing right
it’s hard to see the good

entailment: Able to recognize own emotional pain and distress (finding)
contradiction: Able to resist dangerous thoughts with difficulty (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how heavy it feels to be on that “stage” and to keep replaying the parts you think you’re missing. It’s understandable that when you’re overwhelmed, the bright spots can seem hard to spot. What are a few small things you’ve done recently that you’re proud of, even if they feel minor?

patient.query: Um, I guess I did manage to get out of bed on time a couple of days last week. That felt sort of good, I suppose? And I talked to a friend I hadn't seen in a while, which is a step for me. But then I think about how those are just small things, like they don't really mean much in the grand scheme.

triggers: getting out of bed on time
automatic_thoughts: those are just small things
they don't really mean much
behaviors: managed to get out of bed

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing some progress, but then you feel it’s not that significant. What makes you think these steps are small? Could there be other ways to see their value?

patient.query: I don’t know, maybe I just... I guess I tend to downplay things to avoid feeling let down later. Like, if I get my hopes up about something and it doesn’t turn out great, it just feels heavier. So it’s easier to brush off those little wins. I mean, even just getting through the day can feel like a lot sometimes. But I want to feel good about it, you know? It’s just confusing.

automatic_thoughts: downplaying things
avoiding feeling let down
brushing off little wins

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear you saying that you often downplay achievements to protect yourself from disappointment, and that can feel confusing and heavy. It’s understandable to want to feel good about your day, especially when it feels like a lot. Have you noticed any moments when you did let yourself recognize a small win, and how did that feel?

patient.query: I’m not sure... I mean, there might have been times where I felt a bit proud, like when I finished a project at work. But then I just turned around and thought about how many more I still had to do. It’s like, the good feeling comes and goes so quickly, and then I’m back to stressing about what’s next. It’s frustrating because I want to hold on to the good moments, but they just slip away so fast.

automatic_thoughts: how many more I still had to do
the good feeling comes and goes so quickly
I want to hold on to the good moments

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel proud when you finish a project, but then your thoughts quickly shift to the next task and the pride feels fleeting. You might notice that the pride is still present, even if it’s briefly eclipsed by worry. What small ways could you remind yourself of that pride before the next task takes over?

patient.query: I’m not sure... I mean, there might have been times where I felt a bit proud, like when I finished a project at work. But then I just turned around and thought about how many more I still had to do. It’s like the good feeling comes and goes so quickly, and then I’m back to stressing about what’s next. It’s frustrating because I want to hold on to the good moments, but they just slip away so fast.

automatic_thoughts: how many more I still had to do
the good feeling comes and goes quickly
stressing about what’s next

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught between the satisfaction of finishing a task and the pull to keep moving forward, which can feel exhausting. What does that shift from pride to “what’s next” look like for you in the moment? Have you noticed any times when you were able to pause and savor the accomplishment, and if so, what was different then?

patient.query: Yeah, it definitely feels like I’m on this treadmill, always running but never really getting anywhere. I guess there might have been a couple of times when I took a moment to celebrate something, but it was kind of like a fleeting thought, you know? Like, I’d tell myself, “Good job,” but then jump right back into my to-do list. I don’t know, maybe I just feel guilty for taking a break. It’s almost like I have to earn that moment,

triggers: feeling like I'm on a treadmill
automatic_thoughts: I'm always running but never getting anywhere
I feel guilty for taking a break
I have to earn that moment
behaviors: jumping back into my to-do list

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel you have to earn the time you give yourself. What evidence do you have that taking a break is something you need to earn? How might you view a brief pause differently if you treated it as a deserved reward rather than a debt?

patient.query: I guess it’s just how I grew up, you know? Like, hard work was always the focus, and if you weren’t busy, you weren’t doing enough. So, it makes sense to me to think I need to justify any little break I take. But... if I tried to see it as something I deserve, that’s hard to imagine. It feels almost... wrong? Like taking a break sounds nice, but I can’t shake the thought that it means I’m slacking

triggers: growing up with a focus on hard work
automatic_thoughts: I need to justify any little break I take
taking a break means I'm slacking

neutral: Loss of interest in previously enjoyable activity (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure from your upbringing, and that pressure is making it hard to see breaks as a normal part of life.  
What would happen if you treated a pause as a deserved rest rather than a sign of slacking?  
Can you think of any times when a break actually helped you feel more productive or focused afterward?
